comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the use of Starlink satellite internet on flights, with users sharing experiences, technical questions, and opinions on its impact and future. Some express enthusiasm about the improved connectivity, while others critique the reliance on expensive satellite technology versus traditional infrastructure.

comments.author: Hansenq
comments.text:
Ben Thompson interviewed UA's CEO on Starlink a few months ago.

Scott said: "It took time to negotiate, because we wanted to own the consumer data, and at the beginning, Starlink did, so that was hard, and then, the other thing was I wanted to let my big competitors in the United States finish their deals with other providers and get locked in so that we would — eventually, everyone’s going to have Starlink."

Brilliant. Just brilliant. Ensured that UA would be first (of the 3 major US carriers) to Starlink and that everyone else had to wait until their existing agreements multi-year expired before switching. UA's best CEO in decades!

https://stratechery.com/2026/an-interview-with-united-ceo-sc...
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed, sourced insight into United Airlines' strategic approach to Starlink adoption, supported by an external interview link, making it highly credible.

comments.author: neilsharma425
comments.text:
Neat problem to work on. The tail number lookup is the hard part and it sounds like you solved it the right way, by finding the people who actually track this obsessively rather than trying to scrape it yourself.

Two questions: how stale does the tail assignment data get in practice, and do you have a way to detect when an enthusiast spreadsheet goes unmaintained? And what happens to your probability estimate when an airline swaps aircraft last minute, which seems to happen pretty often on regional routes?
comments.analysis: The comment thoughtfully probes technical challenges in tracking Starlink availability on flights, indicating a good understanding of the problem domain.

comments.author: rayiner
comments.text: I tried Starlink on a United flight the other day (short hop from Hilton Head to DC) and it was amazing.
comments.analysis: A firsthand user experience praising Starlink's performance on a flight, which adds practical perspective though anecdotal.

comments.author: apitman
comments.text: I've only had it once, but inflight Starlink is a game changer. I was able to play a ranked AoE2 game over the Pacific Ocean.
comments.analysis: Another personal account highlighting the low latency and reliability of Starlink in flight, supporting the technology's effectiveness.

comments.author: HorizonXP
comments.text: This is awesome! I just came back from Cancun with my family, and I was on a WestJet flight. I was taken aback by a) free Wifi and b) how fast it was to support everyone streaming YouTube even. Your tracker let me figure out that it was a WestJet flight; now I know that I have to seek out these flights from now on.
comments.analysis: Provides a positive user experience and shows practical use of the Starlink flight tracker, adding value to the discussion.

comments.author: 6thbit
comments.text:
Why does it work on the plane? are the constant handoffs between satellites not enough to break connections or cause extremely high packet loss for it to suck?

is there a speed at which it would break?
comments.analysis: Raises valid technical questions about the feasibility and limitations of maintaining Starlink connections on fast-moving aircraft.

comments.author: devin
comments.text: I've written it elsewhere, but: it is such a shame that the United States saw fit to run electricity _everywhere_, no matter how rural your location, but instead of do the same for rural internet we had to wait for... a private company to launch a global network of satellites. Yes, this post is about internet access while traveling 500mph, which is a different problem, but it is so messed up that people fall over themselves about Starlink for rural connectivity when it is an incredibly complex and expensive technology with huge ongoing costs that could have been solved once and for all by simply running some wires.
comments.analysis: Offers a critical perspective on infrastructure priorities and the high cost of satellite internet, though somewhat tangential to inflight use.

comments.author: aeblyve
comments.text: This is awesome! In the past I would use the promise of starlink or other LEO internet as a tiebreaker for booking flights and was disappointed a few times (as clearly not all of the airframes for an airline have the capability)
comments.analysis: Shares a practical user insight about variability in Starlink availability across aircraft, highlighting real-world limitations.

comments.author: Bombthecat
comments.text:
My trip from USA to Amsterdam doesn't have starlink, at all. Not a single plane. No matter the company.

So sad
comments.analysis: A personal observation about lack of Starlink coverage on a specific route, reflecting current deployment gaps.

comments.author: nomilk
comments.text: It would be great to make this data into a browser extension that overlays the info when using Google Flights
comments.analysis: A constructive suggestion to improve user experience by integrating Starlink flight data with popular flight search tools.

comments.author: torcete
comments.text: People are so rude with their phones that I fear that starlink becomes popular in all flights.
comments.analysis: An emotional comment expressing social annoyance rather than contributing meaningful insight about Starlink technology.

comments.author: throwaway132448
comments.text: No internet on flights is one of my favourite features.
comments.analysis: A personal preference statement that does not add substantive information to the discussion about Starlink.
